text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Cybersecurity and Secure AI Use lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Secure AI use requires approved environments, access controls, vendor security review, audit logs, prompt-injection awareness, data-loss prevention, and incident escalation.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Secure AI use requires approved environments, access controls, vendor security review, audit logs, prompt-injection awareness, data-loss prevention, and incident escalation.
textPreview: Secure AI use requires approved environments, access controls, vendor security review, audit logs, prompt-injection awareness, data-loss ...

text: Cybersecurity protects public health operations, sensitive data, system integrity, and community trust.
textPreview: Cybersecurity protects public health operations, sensitive data, system integrity, and community trust.

text: AI tools can create new attack surfaces, data leakage pathways, and social engineering risks if security is not built into implementation.
textPreview: AI tools can create new attack surfaces, data leakage pathways, and social engineering risks if security is not built into implementation.

text:
Use this slide in the Cybersecurity and Secure AI Use training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 2, 3, 7, 11, 12 and Tools 10, 15, 17, 34, 39, 43.
Emphasize the core guardrail: AI tools can create new attack surfaces, data leakage pathways, and social engineering risks if security is not built into implementation.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Cybersecurity and Secure AI Use in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Cybersecurity and Secure AI Use training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 2, 3, 7, 11, 12 and Tools 10, 15, 17, 34, 39, 43.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

AI security is broader than protecting the model. It includes user accounts, data flows, integrations, APIs, vendor hosting, prompts, outputs, logs, plugins, browser extensions, and connections to existing systems. Staff need to know how secure AI use differs from general software use.

Use this slide in the Cybersecurity and Secure AI Use training module.
Use this slide to provide the narrative overview and definition before moving into detailed application.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 2, 3, 7, 11, 12 and Tools 10, 15, 17, 34, 39, 43.
Ask the group to restate the topic in their own words.

Sensitive data entered into unapproved tools or retained by vendors. Prompt injection or malicious content that manipulates AI behavior. Unauthorized access through weak role controls, shared accounts, or unmanaged integrations. Unverified.
textPreview: Sensitive data entered into unapproved tools or retained by vendors. Prompt injection or malicious content that manipulates AI behavior. ...

text: Use only approved AI tools for official work. Apply role-based access, multi-factor authentication, logging, data-loss prevention, and least-privilege access. Review vendor security documentation, hosting, subcontractors, breach notification,.

text: Include security review before pilot approval. Test integrations in controlled environments. Coordinate AI incident response with cybersecurity and privacy response plans.

Start with Security Concerns, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

Use this slide in the Cybersecurity and Secure AI Use training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 2, 3, 7, 11, 12 and Tools 10, 15, 17, 34, 39, 43.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
Which AI tools are approved for official work? Which integrations or data flows require security review? How should staff report suspicious AI behavior, data leakage, or unauthorized access?

Expected takeaways include: AI security review notes, Approved-use rules, Security escalation checklist.
